chunk_summary: This section discusses age-specific feeding management for children with neurodisability, emphasizing developmental needs, texture progression, standardized plans, and transition strategies for autonomy in adolescence.
learning_objectives: Understand feeding assessment priorities for infants and toddlers with neurodisability, including suck-swallow-breathe coordination and caregiver techniques.
Learn how to standardize feeding plans using EDACS and IDDSI frameworks for school-age children.
Develop strategies for transitioning adolescents with neurodisability to autonomous feeding or tube management.
key_facts: Complementary foods should be introduced around 6 months, per WHO guidelines, with texture progression matching motor skills.
Use Mini-EDACS for tracking biting, chewing, and texture handling in children aged 18-36 months.
IDDSI levels and bedside instructions should standardize feeding plans for school-age children across home, school, and therapy settings.
Adolescents transitioning to autonomy should have a portable feeding summary and device emergency plan.
Tube-fed youth should learn self-management skills, including site care, flushing, and response to dislodgement.
reference_quote: Complementary foods should still be introduced around 6 months per WHO guidance, but texture progression should match motor skills. (Reference: WHO Guiding Principles for Complementary Feeding of the Breastfed Child)
document_key_topics: Feeding assessment and management for infants and toddlers with neurodisability
Standardized feeding plans for school-age children using EDACS and IDDSI
Transition planning for adolescents with neurodisability
